core_concept: Telling Time to the Minute and Calculating Elapsed Time
facts: On an analog clock, the minute hand pointing at the 3 means 15 minutes past the hour because each number represents 5 minutes and 3 × 5 = 15.

Elapsed time is the duration between a start time and an end time; for example, from 10:30 AM to 11:15 AM is 45 minutes because counting up by 5-minute intervals on a number line gives 30 minutes to 11:00 then 15 more minutes.

When calculating elapsed time that crosses from AM to PM, such as from 10:45 AM to 1:15 PM, you must add the time from start to noon (1 hour 15 minutes) plus from noon to end (1 hour 15 minutes) to get a total of 2 hours 30 minutes.

A digital clock displays time in hours and minutes using digits; reading to the minute is direct because the last two digits show the exact minute, unlike an analog clock where the minute hand's position relative to tick marks determines the minute.
misconceptions: Students think elapsed time can be found by subtracting the minutes and hours separately without regrouping; e.g., they calculate from 10:50 to 11:10 as 40 minutes because they subtract 50 from 10 and get -40 then take the absolute value, instead of counting up 10 minutes to 11:00 and then 10 more to 11:10.

When reading an analog clock, students often read the minute hand as the hour hand and vice versa, so a clock showing 3:15 (hour hand just past 3, minute hand on 3) is misread as 12:03 because they think the long hand points to the hour and the short hand to the minute.
